FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline data from 10,842 participants across 21 sites (ages 9-10)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis comparing autism vs. typically developing children on screen time and obesity

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original research published in Frontiers in Pediatrics

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 search successful: Four specific phone/SM variables with numeric estimates paired with obesity (individual-trait health outcome): (1) passive viewing (TV/movies/videos): hours/day with prevalence ratios; (2) video games: minutes/day with prevalence ratios; (3) social media/video chat: minutes/day with prevalence ratios (p=0.58 overall but significant by autism status); (4) texting: minutes/day with prevalence ratios (p=0.04). Other side is obesity status (binary health outcome = individual-trait-level)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results: prevalence ratios with 95% confidence intervals, p-values, and stratified analyses by autism status and gender (Tables 3, 4, 5)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results for SM/phone-obesity associations: e.g., passive viewing p=0.002; video games p=0.05; texting p=0.04 overall; stratified by autism status with individual prevalence ratios

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design (baseline data only); EC7 exclusion rule requires longitudinal design with SM as DV in every analysis and no SM-as-IV analysis. This paper is cross-sectional, so EC7 does not apply
notes: Paper analyzes baseline ABCD data comparing screen time patterns by autism status (1.7% with autism, 98.3% typically developing) and associations with obesity. Clear Step 1 hits on four modalities (passive viewing, video games, social media/video chat, texting) each with separate quantitative estimates paired with obesity outcome

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=8,324 children at baseline (ages 108-131 months, ~9-11 years); follow-up N=3,891 at year 2

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis of ABCD data examining resting-state fMRI connectivity and screen time effects

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original research published in Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Daily screen time measured as composite. Step 2 composite-content check: Methods state daily screen time includes 'watching TV shows or movies, watching videos, and playing video games' plus texting, video chatting, and social media (section 2.2.2). This composite bundles TV/movies (non-phone modality) with phone/SM modalities without separate breakout. Per binding clarifications: 'Multi-device aggregated time fails Step 2' and 'composite includes non-phone/non-SM ingredient (TV, console gaming, PC gaming, unspecified gaming, music, reading on screens, computer-only) without separate breakout → NOT_MET.' TV is explicitly non-phone. The paper reports composite-level results only with no per-modality breakdown, confirming bundling without separate estimates for phone/SM only

notes: Paper uses Screen-Activity Proportion (SAP) as composite measure of daily screen time including TV, videos, video games, texting, video chatting, and social media. While the outcome (fronto-striatal connectivity, an individual-trait neural measure) is appropriate, the exposure composite explicitly bundles television (a non-phone-based activity) with phone/SM activities. The criteria require Step 1 to find phone/SM-specific estimates; the paper reports only composite-level results. This fails FT-IC2 Step 2 due to non-phone bundling without breakout

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=11,633 at baseline (ages 9-11 years, 2016-2018 recruitment); two-year follow-up data (2018-2020)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis examining associations between baseline screen time and suicidal behaviors at two-year follow-up

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original research published in Preventive Medicine

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 search successful: Four specific phone/SM-modality variables with numeric estimates paired with suicidal behaviors (individual-trait behavioral/mental health outcome): (1) texting: aOR 1.36, 95% CI 1.06-1.74; (2) video chatting: aOR 1.30, 95% CI 1.03-1.65; (3) watching videos: aOR 1.21, 95% CI 1.04-1.39; (4) playing video games: aOR 1.18, 95% CI 1.01-1.38. Also reports total screen time aOR 1.09 per hour. Other side is suicidal behaviors (passive ideation, active ideation with intent, or suicide attempt) assessed via KSADS-5, a gold-standard diagnostic instrument measuring individual-trait behavioral outcomes

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: odds ratios (aOR) with 95% confidence intervals and p-values for each modality. Total screen time: aOR 1.09 (95% CI 1.03-1.14, p<0.001); texting: aOR 1.36 (95% CI 1.06-1.74); video chatting: aOR 1.30 (95% CI 1.03-1.65); watching videos: aOR 1.21 (95% CI 1.04-1.39); video games: aOR 1.18 (95% CI 1.01-1.38)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results for prospective SM/phone-to-suicidal-behavior associations: adjusted odds ratios with confidence intervals for total and modality-specific screen time predicting two-year-follow-up suicidal behaviors (abstract and Table 2)

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design (baseline screen time predicts two-year-follow-up suicidal behaviors), but smartphone/SM is the IV in all analyses, NOT the DV. EC7 applies only when SM is DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV analysis. Here, screen time is the IV (exposure) and suicidal behaviors are the DV (outcome), so EC7 does not apply
notes: Prospective cohort study with strong Step 1 IC2 hits: four separate modality-specific estimates (texting, video chatting, watching videos, video games) each independently associated with suicidal behaviors. Clear individual-trait outcome (suicidal behaviors measured via gold-standard KSADS-5 instrument assessing passive ideation, active ideation with intent, or attempt). Screen time appears as IV in all analyses, not DV
